title: The Tudors: Reformation and Elizabeth I
subject: history
courseDescription: Henry VIII's break with Rome, the English Reformation and the reign of Elizabeth I.
lesson:
The Tudors: Reformation and Elizabeth I

Key facts
Level: GCSE
Theme: British History
Period: 1509 — 1603

Key question. How did the Tudors change England's religion and turn it into a confident nation?

Henry VIII broke with the Pope and launched the English Reformation, making himself head of the Church. After his children Edward VI and Catholic Mary I, Elizabeth I settled religion with a moderate 'middle way' and defeated the Spanish Armada in 1588.

1 · Henry VIII and the break with Rome

When the Pope refused to annul his marriage to Catherine of Aragon, Henry broke away. The Act of Supremacy (1534) made him head of the Church of England, and he dissolved the monasteries, seizing their wealth.

Term · The Reformation
The split of western Christianity into Catholic and Protestant. In England it began politically, when Henry VIII rejected the Pope's authority — deeper Protestant belief spread later.

2 · Mary I and Elizabeth I

Henry's children pulled England in different directions. Catholic Mary I ('Bloody Mary') burned Protestants to restore the old faith. Then Elizabeth I chose a moderate Religious Settlement — a Protestant 'middle way' to unite the country.

3 · Elizabeth's England

Elizabeth reigned for 45 years. She faced the Catholic threat of Mary, Queen of Scots (executed 1587) and defeated the Spanish Armada in 1588. Her age saw explorers like Francis Drake and the plays of William Shakespeare — a golden age.

In summary

Timeline

DateEvent
1509Henry VIII becomes king
1534Act of Supremacy (break with Rome)
1553-58Mary I restores Catholicism
1558Elizabeth I becomes queen
1587Mary, Queen of Scots executed
1588Defeat of the Spanish Armada

Key terms

TermMeaning
ReformationSplit into Catholic and Protestant
Act of SupremacyMade the king head of the Church
DissolutionClosing of the monasteries
Religious SettlementElizabeth's moderate 'middle way'
Spanish ArmadaSpanish invasion fleet of 1588

People

NameRole
Henry VIIIBroke with Rome
Mary IRestored Catholicism harshly
Elizabeth IProtestant 'middle way'; beat the Armada
Mary, Queen of ScotsCatholic rival, executed 1587
Watch out
Henry VIII was not a committed Protestant: he broke with Rome mainly for political and personal reasons (his marriage and power). And don't mix up the sisters — Mary I forced Catholicism harshly, while Elizabeth I chose a moderate Protestant settlement.

In under a century, the Tudors remade England — its Church, its monarchy and its place in the world. The confident, Protestant nation of Elizabeth I would go on to build an empire.

cards.recto: Which Tudor king broke England away from the Roman Catholic Church?
cards.explanation: When the Pope refused to annul his marriage to Catherine of Aragon, Henry broke with Rome and made himself head of the Church in England.

cards.recto: Why did Henry VIII first want to break with the Pope?
cards.verso: The Pope refused to annul his marriage to Catherine of Aragon
cards.explanation: Henry wanted a male heir and a new wife, Anne Boleyn. When the Pope would not end his first marriage, Henry took control of the Church himself.

medium: He was ordered to by Parliament
hard: He wished to become Pope himself
He wanted to end all taxes

cards.recto: What 1534 law made Henry VIII the head of the Church of England?
cards.explanation: It declared the king, not the Pope, Supreme Head of the Church in England. This was the legal heart of the English Reformation.

hard: The Statute in Restraint of Appeals

cards.recto: What did Henry VIII do to the monasteries in the 1530s?
cards.verso: He dissolved (closed) them and seized their wealth
cards.explanation: The Dissolution of the Monasteries shut down hundreds of religious houses. Henry took their land and riches, boosting royal income.

easy: He gave them to the Pope
He turned them into schools for all
medium: He made them royal palaces only
hard: He sold them back to Rome
He converted them into castles only

cards.recto: The break with Rome and the rise of Protestant ideas in England is called the...?
cards.explanation: The Reformation split western Christianity into Catholic and Protestant. In England it began politically under Henry VIII.

cards.recto: Which Catholic queen, Henry's daughter, persecuted Protestants and earned the nickname 'Bloody Mary'?
cards.explanation: Mary tried to restore Catholicism and had hundreds of Protestants burned. Her reign was short and left England divided.

cards.recto: Which Protestant queen, daughter of Henry VIII and Anne Boleyn, reigned from 1558 to 1603?
cards.explanation: Elizabeth restored Protestantism and ruled for 45 years, a period of relative stability, exploration and cultural achievement.

cards.recto: What was Elizabeth I's moderate religious policy, balancing Catholic and Protestant, called?
cards.explanation: Elizabeth made England Protestant but kept some familiar features to avoid conflict — a 'middle way' meant to unite most people.

cards.recto: Which Catholic rival did Elizabeth eventually have executed in 1587?
cards.explanation: Mary was a Catholic claimant to Elizabeth's throne and a focus for plots. After years of imprisonment, Elizabeth had her executed.

cards.recto: What great fleet did Elizabethan England defeat in 1588?
cards.explanation: Catholic Spain sent a huge fleet to invade England. Storms and English tactics scattered it, a famous victory that boosted Elizabeth's prestige.

cards.recto: Which country sent the Armada against Protestant England in 1588?
cards.explanation: Catholic Spain, led by Philip II, wanted to overthrow Protestant Elizabeth. Its defeat became a symbol of English resistance.

cards.recto: [Compare] How did the religious policies of Mary I and Elizabeth I differ?
cards.verso: Mary I forced a return to Catholicism harshly; Elizabeth I chose a moderate Protestant 'middle way'
cards.explanation: Mary persecuted Protestants to restore Catholicism. Elizabeth made England Protestant again but sought compromise to keep the peace.

medium: Mary was moderate; Elizabeth was harsh
hard: Mary chose a middle way; Elizabeth forced Catholicism
Both abolished the monarchy's church role

cards.recto: [Watch out] Was the English Reformation mainly caused by religious belief or by politics?
cards.verso: It began mainly for political and personal reasons under Henry VIII, not deep Protestant belief
cards.explanation: A common error is to think Henry was a committed Protestant. In fact he broke with Rome over his marriage and power; Protestant belief spread more later.

easy: Henry was a lifelong devout Protestant
It had nothing to do with the king
It was ordered by the Pope
medium: It was purely about translating the Bible
It began under Elizabeth, not Henry
It was caused by the Armada
hard: Henry adopted Luther's theology fully in 1534
It was driven only by ordinary people

cards.recto: Which explorer sailed around the world and raided Spanish ships for Elizabeth I?
cards.explanation: Drake circumnavigated the globe and attacked Spanish treasure ships. To England he was a hero; to Spain, a pirate.

cards.recto: Which famous playwright wrote during the Elizabethan 'golden age' of theatre?
cards.explanation: Shakespeare's plays, performed at theatres like the Globe, made the Elizabethan era a high point of English culture.

cards.recto: Overall, why is the Tudor period a turning point in English history?
cards.verso: England broke with Rome, became Protestant and grew as a confident sea power under Elizabeth I
cards.explanation: The Reformation reshaped religion and royal power, while Elizabeth's reign brought stability, exploration and cultural greatness — setting England on a new path.

medium: England joined the Holy Roman Empire
hard: The Pope regained control of England
